question: According to the Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (MHRA, 2004), what is the explicit core definition of 'Medicines Management'?
options: The rapid prescription of innovative pharmaceutical compounds to reduce general ward length of stay markers
The clinical, cost effective and safe use of medicines to ensure patients get the maximum benefit from the medicines they need, while at the same time minimising potential harm
The total automation of distribution networks to minimize independent clinical decision-making paths
The localized logging of adverse prescription metrics to fulfill international legislative datasets exclusively

question: How does the Department of Health, Northern Ireland, define the ultimate 'goal' of safe and effective medicines management?
options: To optimize the benefits that treatment offers and attain the best outcome for each patient
To contract the aggregate overhead expenditure tied to baseline ward fluid stocks
To accelerate independent prescribing pathways for student nurse cohorts
To standardize pharmaceutical manufacturing brands across the health service footprint • • • • Salford Professional Practice Centre • Exam Paper 3 of 20 Page 1 of 8

question: The lecture slides outline five distinct reasons why nurses are actively involved in Medicines Management. Which of the following is explicitly listed?
options: Budgetary oversight management
Front line patient care and safe drug calculation
Independent pharmacological formulation
Direct regulatory adjustment of national formularies

question: Under the student nurse guidelines slide, what are the three explicit regulatory framework requirements named that support safe medicines management?
options: NMC Standards of Proficiency (2018), The Code (2018), A Competency Framework for all Prescribers (2016)
The Care Act (2014), Human Medicines Regulations (2012), Health and Safety at Work Act (1974)
NICE Guideline 174, Waterlow Index, MUST Screening Protocol
Salford Ward Directives, Greater Manchester Formulary Guide, Datix Logging Matrix

question: The section of the Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) Code on preserving safety explicitly mandates that medicines must be administered:
options: Immediately upon supervisor request regardless of independent knowledge checks
Within the limits of your training and competence, the law, our guidance and other relevant policies, guidance and regulations
Exclusively utilizing the trade or brand name listed across local ward cabinets
By student nurses independently once they reach their second placement calendar tier

question: Under '1. Right Person' within the 10 Rights (10 Rs) framework, what specific clinical warning is emphasized regarding patient characteristics?
options: Keep in mind names can be similar and people may have the same birthday
Verify room assignments as the sole method of confirming an individual's presence
Assume a patient who does not reply is giving unspoken confirmation
Rely entirely on the verbal direction of an emergency coordinator Salford Professional Practice Centre • Exam Paper 3 of 20 Page 2 of 8

question: Under '2. Right Medicine', what explicit visual care rule is highlighted to avoid error?
options: Rely on package color coding schemas across stock boxes
Double-check labels, being especially careful with drugs that have similar names to others
Prioritize brand designations over generic structural naming bands
Dispense from unnamed storage cups if a colleague confirms the contents verbally

question: Under '5. Right Time', what two practical elements must a nurse actively check or consider prior to drug delivery?
options: The total hours remaining on their shift and general ward meal timelines
Whether the medicine needs to be taken before or after food, and if the interval since the last dose is correct
The age of the admitting practitioner and the daily calendar date block
The standard duration of the localized ward handover meeting sequence

question: What is the absolute, unalterable rule outlined under '10. Right Documentation' regarding when a drug must be recorded?
options: Record immediately BEFORE giving the drug, to ensure the chart is pre-validated
Record immediately AFTER the drug is given, NEVER BEFORE GIVING THE DRUG
Log all administered items in a single task sequence at the absolute close of the shift window
Sign the chart only after a peer provides verbal confirmation from the nurse's station

question: Why does the lecture slide warn against ever documenting a medication before it has actually been swallowed or received by the patient?
options: Because the chart system automatically logs out if delayed
Because you may get called away and be prevented from doing so
Because it modifies the digital pharmacokinetics timeline data points
Because prescribers require active real-time notification alerts Salford Professional Practice Centre • Exam Paper 3 of 20 Page 3 of 8

question: What explicit physical action must a nurse perform to fully validate and co-verify a medication's true administration status?
options: Leave the medicine pot on the locker table and return in thirty minutes
Stay with your patient until you have seen them take the medication
Entrust the administration to a visiting family relative who is at the bedside
Check the room number against the central ward roster portal mapping

question: The lecture underscores two vital legal and professional maxims regarding clinical documentation records. What are they?
options: 'If it is written in pencil, it is temporary' and 'The chart is an internal guide'
'If it has not been recorded, you haven’t given it' and 'Remember that the medicines chart is a legal document'
'Verbal reporting overrides chart signatures' and 'Omissions do not require notation'
'Students are exempt from legal logging rules' and 'Co-signing is an optional practice'

question: In Case Scenario 1, a supervising nurse gives a second-year student a medicine pot containing 3 tablets and tells them to give it to a patient in bay 4 because of an emergency. Can the student administer this?
options: Yes, because emergency contexts temporarily suspend standard supervision rules
No, because the student has no prescription context, did not prepare the drug, and must work under direct supervision
Yes, provided the student visually double-checks the tablet colors in the pot
No, unless the patient verbally names the three medications independently Salford Professional Practice Centre • Exam Paper 3 of 20 Page 4 of 8

question: In Case Scenario 2, Nurse Jenny enters a four-bed bay containing two patients named Jack. What initial identification failure occurs?
options: She checks the wrong wristband code string
She asks verbally, 'Jack, are you ready for your tablets?' without confirming full identity or checking the name band
She reviews a chart that belongs to a completely different ward sector
She relies on a family member's incorrect structural nod

question: During the Case Scenario 2 events, what active choice did patient Jack Dixon make when Jenny approached his bedside locker?
options: He explicitly stated that he was not ready for his therapy
He looked up from reading his newspaper but did not reply
He signed the chart form on Jenny's behalf
He swallowed the tablets without realizing they were incorrect

question: What action did Jenny execute immediately after leaving the medicine pot on Jack Dixon's bedside locker?
options: She waited ten minutes to monitor for potential adverse effects
She inaccurately recorded the medication as administered, even though the patient did not take it, and moved on
She cross-referenced his wristband against the prescription sheet profile
She contacted the prescribing clinician to report an independent refusal

question: How was Jenny's administrative error discovered thirty minutes later?
options: Through an automated system audit flag on the digital charts
Jack Dixon returned the pot to the nurse in charge at the nurse’s station, stating he hadn't taken them and noticed 'the little blue one' was missing
Jack Stewart experienced an acute physiological deterioration episode
The ward supervisor performed a random stock count check across the bay Salford Professional Practice Centre • Exam Paper 3 of 20 Page 5 of 8

question: What was the final outcome of Jenny's actions for the patients in that bay?
options: Jack Dixon received a double dose of his scheduled daily therapy
As a result, both patients named Jack have not received the correct medication
The error was automatically averted with zero impact on records or care maps
Jack Stewart received his daily tablets via an alternate clinical route

question: The NMC guidelines explicitly state what strict directive regarding student nurses and medicinal products?
options: Students may administer oral medications independently starting in their final year
Student nurses must NEVER administer or supply medicinal products without DIRECT supervision
Students are authorized to verify prescriptions under indirect peer overview schemas
Emergency floor conditions invalidate standard supervision tracking rules

question: What mandatory requirement is outlined regarding medication charts during student-led administration rounds?
options: The student signs independently and logs an audit note at shift end
A qualified nurse must always supervise medication administration and co-sign the medication chart
The patient must sign a localized consent line inside the paper book
Co-signing is only requested if a complex fluid or injection is prepared

question: If a medication is intentionally not given to a patient during a round, what step must be executed?
options: Leave the chart completely blank to signal an omission to the next shift tier
Documenting Omissions: If a medication is not given, record the reason in the medication chart or patient’s notes
Inform the clinical supervisor verbally without making any formal entry marks
Destroy the physical dose cup and adjust the generic room log numbers Salford Professional Practice Centre • Exam Paper 3 of 20 Page 6 of 8

question: What must a nurse do if they identify an absolute reason not to give a medication or discover an improper prescription?
options: Modify the prescription entries manually on the patient's chart sheet
Contacting the Prescriber: Inform the prescriber immediately and clarify before administering
Administer the medication anyway to preserve continuity of care curves
Delegate the round to a peer to avoid personal legal accountability lines

question: What core directive is highlighted under the 'Medication Handling' section to avoid clinical error?
options: Pre-pour all scheduled morning tablets to save time during high-occupancy shifts
Never leave medications unattended or ungiven—ensure the patient takes them
Store prepared medication pots at the nurse's station during emergency briefs
Always pass multi-tablet pots to an adjacent patient if a mismatch is assumed

question: Which of the following contains the correct, complete list of multi-faceted roles filled by a nurse in medicines management as displayed in the closing slides?
options: Inspector, Prescriber, Technician, Clerk, Roster Coordinator, Safety Officer
Communicator, Advocate, Educator, Partner, Assessor, Administrator, Evaluator, Carer, Nurse
Diagnostician, Surgeon, Advisor, Pharmacist, Legal Director, Ward Auditor
Manager, Analyst, Compliance Representative, Supply Officer, Gatekeeper

question: Which historical publication is cited in your reference list for providing specific guidance titled 'How to calculate drug dosage accurately: advice for nurses'?
options: Elliott et al. (2020)
Haigh, S. (2002) in the Nursing Times
Murphy, M. (2012)
Pearce, L. (2021) Salford Professional Practice Centre • Exam Paper 3 of 20 Page 7 of 8
